content.text: Embedding models have a fixed input limit (typically 512–8192 tokens). Source documents are almost always longer. Chunking splits them into pieces that fit the embedding window. But the split point matters enormously: a chunk that ends mid-sentence loses the sentence's meaning, and a chunk that is too large dilutes the embedding signal with irrelevant surrounding text. The goal is chunks that are semantically coherent — each one should contain a single idea — so the embedding accurately represents what the chunk is about.

attrs.alt: Chunking strategies compared: fixed-size splits mid-sentence, recursive respects boundaries, semantic groups by topic; overlap diagram shows context preservation
attrs.title: Chunking Strategies

content.text: RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter is the right default for most prose. It tries a prioritised list of separators — paragraph breaks first, then newlines, then sentence endings, then word boundaries — and only falls back to a hard character split if no natural boundary exists within the size limit. This produces chunks that almost never split mid-sentence.

from langchain_text_splitters import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter
from langchain_core.documents import Document

splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(
    chunk_size=512,
    chunk_overlap=64,
    separators=["\n\n", "\n", ". ", " "],
    length_function=len,
)

text = """Return Policy

Items may be returned within 30 days of purchase. All returns must include
the original receipt. Damaged items are not eligible for return.

Shipping Policy

We ship to all 50 states. Standard shipping takes 5-7 business days.
Express shipping is available for an additional fee."""

doc = Document(page_content=text, metadata={"source": "policy.txt"})
chunks = splitter.split_documents([doc])
for i, chunk in enumerate(chunks):
    print(f"Chunk {i}: {len(chunk.page_content)} chars | {chunk.page_content[:80]!r}")

Setting chunk_size in characters is imprecise: English text averages ~4 characters per token, but code, URLs, and non-English text can be 2–10 characters per token. When the chunk must fit within a specific token limit (e.g., an embedding model's 512-token max), pass a tiktoken-based length function so chunk_size is measured in tokens exactly.

import tiktoken
from langchain_text_splitters import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter

enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model("gpt-4o")

def token_len(text: str) -> int:
    return len(enc.encode(text))

token_splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(
    chunk_size=256,
    chunk_overlap=32,
    length_function=token_len,
    separators=["\n\n", "\n", ". ", " "],
)

sample = "The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. " * 200
chunks = token_splitter.split_text(sample)
print(f"Split into {len(chunks)} chunks")
for i, c in enumerate(chunks[:3]):
    print(f"  Chunk {i}: {token_len(c)} tokens")

Documentation and wikis written in Markdown have a natural hierarchy (H1 > H2 > H3). MarkdownHeaderTextSplitter uses these headings as natural boundaries and promotes them to chunk metadata. Each chunk's metadata records which H1, H2, and H3 it belongs to, enabling metadata filtering like "only retrieve chunks under the Authentication section."

from langchain_text_splitters import MarkdownHeaderTextSplitter

markdown_doc = """
# API Reference

## Authentication

Use Bearer tokens for all API requests. Tokens expire after 24 hours.

### Refreshing Tokens

Call POST /auth/refresh with your existing token to get a new one.

## Endpoints

### GET /users

Returns a paginated list of users. Requires admin scope.
"""

headers_to_split_on = [("#", "h1"), ("##", "h2"), ("###", "h3")]
splitter = MarkdownHeaderTextSplitter(headers_to_split_on=headers_to_split_on)
chunks = splitter.split_text(markdown_doc)

for chunk in chunks:
    print(chunk.metadata, "->", chunk.page_content[:60])
# {"h1": "API Reference", "h2": "Authentication"} -> Use Bearer tokens...
# {"h1": "API Reference", "h2": "Authentication", "h3": "Refreshing Tokens"} -> Call POST /auth/refresh...

Small chunks produce precise embeddings but lose surrounding context when retrieved; large chunks preserve context but dilute the embedding. Parent-document retrieval resolves this by indexing small child chunks for embedding precision and returning larger parent chunks to the LLM. The vector search finds the right small chunk; the retriever then fetches its parent, giving the model full context.

from langchain_community.vectorstores import Chroma
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
from langchain.retrievers import ParentDocumentRetriever
from langchain.storage import InMemoryStore
from langchain_text_splitters import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter
from langchain_core.documents import Document

child_splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(chunk_size=200, chunk_overlap=20)
parent_splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(chunk_size=1000, chunk_overlap=100)

vectorstore = Chroma(
    collection_name="child_chunks",
    embedding_function=OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small"),
)
docstore = InMemoryStore()

retriever = ParentDocumentRetriever(
    vectorstore=vectorstore,
    docstore=docstore,
    child_splitter=child_splitter,
    parent_splitter=parent_splitter,
)

docs = [
    Document(
        page_content=(
            "The return policy allows returns within 30 days. Items must be unused. "
            "Include original receipt. Refunds are processed within 5 business days. "
            "Damaged items are handled separately by the claims team."
        ) * 5,
        metadata={"source": "policy.txt"},
    )
]
retriever.add_documents(docs)

results = retriever.invoke("What is the refund processing time?")
for r in results:
    print(f"{len(r.page_content)} chars from {r.metadata.get('source')}")
# Returns the large parent chunk (~1000 chars) matched via small child chunk embedding

Character-based splitters are unaware of topic shifts within a chunk. SemanticChunker (langchain-experimental) embeds consecutive sentences and splits where the cosine distance between adjacent sentences jumps sharply. This produces topic-coherent chunks of variable size. It is slower than character-based splitting (requires embedding every sentence) but produces higher-quality chunks for mixed-topic documents.

# Semantic chunking: split where the topic changes, detected by embedding distance
# pip install langchain-experimental

from langchain_experimental.text_splitter import SemanticChunker
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings

# breakpoint_threshold_type controls how aggressively to split:
# "percentile" (default): split at the top N% of embedding distance jumps
# "standard_deviation": split when distance exceeds mean + N * std
chunker = SemanticChunker(
    embeddings=OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small"),
    breakpoint_threshold_type="percentile",
    breakpoint_threshold_amount=90,  # split at top 10% of semantic distance jumps
)

text = """
Machine learning is a subset of artificial intelligence that enables systems
to learn from data. Neural networks are a key technique in modern ML.

Photosynthesis is the process by which plants convert sunlight into glucose.
Chlorophyll absorbs light in the red and blue wavelengths.

The French Revolution began in 1789 with the storming of the Bastille.
It transformed French society and had lasting effects on European politics.
"""

chunks = chunker.create_documents([text])
print(f"Semantic chunks: {len(chunks)}")
for i, c in enumerate(chunks):
    print(f"  [{i}] {c.page_content[:80]!r}")
# Each paragraph (topic) becomes its own chunk because embedding distance
# between "ML/neural networks" and "photosynthesis" is large.

Splitting source code at character boundaries bisects function and class definitions, making retrieval nearly useless. RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter.from_language() uses language-specific separators (def, class, function, {, ;) to split at natural code boundaries. Python, JavaScript, TypeScript, Go, Rust, Java, C, and C++ are all supported. The result is chunks that correspond to complete functions or classes rather than arbitrary character runs.

Before committing to a chunk size, measure whether chunks are semantically coherent. A quick proxy is boundary similarity: embed consecutive chunks and compute cosine similarity between adjacent chunks that should be about different topics. Low similarity at the boundary means the splitter cleanly separated topics; high similarity means topics are bleeding across chunks and a smaller chunk_size or semantic splitter would improve precision.

# Evaluating chunk quality: measure chunk coherence and boundary quality
# Two practical checks: average embedding similarity within a chunk (coherence)
# and similarity drop at chunk boundaries (boundary quality).

from langchain_community.vectorstores import Chroma
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
from langchain_text_splitters import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter
from langchain_core.documents import Document
import numpy as np

embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")

text = (
    "Machine learning enables systems to learn from data. "
    "Neural networks are the backbone of modern AI. "
    "Deep learning uses multiple layers to extract features.\n\n"
    "Photosynthesis is how plants convert sunlight to glucose. "
    "Chlorophyll is the pigment responsible for light absorption. "
    "Plants release oxygen as a byproduct of photosynthesis."
)

def cosine_sim(a: list[float], b: list[float]) -> float:
    a, b = np.array(a), np.array(b)
    return float(np.dot(a, b) / (np.linalg.norm(a) * np.linalg.norm(b)))

def measure_boundary_drop(splitter, text: str) -> dict:
    chunks = splitter.split_text(text)
    if len(chunks) < 2:
        return {"chunks": len(chunks), "boundary_drop": None}
    
    vecs = embeddings.embed_documents(chunks)
    within_sim = cosine_sim(vecs[0], vecs[0])   # trivially 1.0 for first chunk
    boundary_sim = cosine_sim(vecs[0], vecs[-1]) # similarity across topic boundary
    return {
        "chunks": len(chunks),
        "boundary_similarity": round(boundary_sim, 3),
        "note": "lower = cleaner topic separation at boundary",
    }

# Small chunks: cleaner boundaries
small = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(chunk_size=200, chunk_overlap=20)
print("Small chunks:", measure_boundary_drop(small, text))

# Large chunks: topics blend together
large = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(chunk_size=600, chunk_overlap=60)
print("Large chunks:", measure_boundary_drop(large, text))

After splitting, a quick audit function catches the three most common problems: chunks that are too short (likely boilerplate, page numbers, or headers that survived cleaning), chunks that are too long (the splitter fell back to a hard split because no separator was found), and chunks that end mid-sentence (a heuristic: the last character is alphabetic rather than punctuation). Fixing these before indexing improves retrieval quality and reduces hallucination.

# Common chunking pitfalls and how to detect them

from langchain_text_splitters import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter
from langchain_core.documents import Document

splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(chunk_size=300, chunk_overlap=30)


def audit_chunks(chunks: list) -> dict:
    """Check for common quality issues across a set of chunks."""
    issues = {
        "too_short": [],    # chunks below 50 chars — probably boilerplate or headers
        "too_long": [],     # chunks exceeding 1.5x chunk_size — splitter fallback
        "mid_sentence": [], # chunks that start/end mid-sentence
    }
    for i, chunk in enumerate(chunks):
        text = chunk if isinstance(chunk, str) else chunk.page_content
        if len(text.strip()) < 50:
            issues["too_short"].append(i)
        if len(text) > 450:   # 1.5 * 300
            issues["too_long"].append(i)
        # Heuristic: chunk ends with a word character (not punctuation) = mid-sentence
        if text.strip() and text.strip()[-1].isalpha():
            issues["mid_sentence"].append(i)
    return issues


doc = Document(
    page_content=(
        "Acme Corp Q3 2024 Report\n\n"
        "Revenue reached $142.3M this quarter, representing an 18% year-over-year "
        "increase. The growth was driven primarily by enterprise sales, which grew "
        "27% to $89M. Consumer revenue declined 3% to $53.3M.\n\n"
        "Gross margin improved to 67% from 63% in Q3 2023, reflecting operating "
        "leverage across the business as headcount grew only 8% while revenue grew 18%."
    ),
    metadata={"source": "q3_report.txt"},
)

chunks = splitter.split_documents([doc])
issues = audit_chunks(chunks)
print(f"Chunks: {len(chunks)}")
print(f"Issues: {issues}")
for i, c in enumerate(chunks):
    print(f"  [{i}] {len(c.page_content)} chars | ends: {c.page_content[-20:]!r}")

RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter is the right default: it respects paragraph and sentence boundaries before falling back to hard character splits. Use tiktoken as the length function when chunk_size must be exact in tokens. Use MarkdownHeaderTextSplitter for structured documentation so heading hierarchy becomes filterable metadata. Use parent-document retrieval when small chunks give precise embeddings but the LLM needs larger context. Use SemanticChunker for mixed-topic documents where character-based splitting would merge unrelated ideas into one chunk.
